title: Quantum algorithm for solving high-dimensional linear stochastic differential equations via amplitude encoding of the noise term
abstract: This work studies quantum algorithms to solve high-dimensional stochastic differential equations (SDEs) $\mathrm{d} \mathbf{X}_t = A(t) \mathbf{X}_t \mathrm{d} t + B(t) \mathrm{d} \mathbf{W}_t$. Aiming for a speed-up in the dimension $N$ of $\mathbf{X}_t$, we generate quantum states that encode $\mathbf{X}_t$ in the amplitudes, while most of the existing quantum methods for SDEs employ binary encoding. A key challenge is the amplitude encoding of the noise term, and we address this by utilizing the quantum circuit implementation of a pseudorandom number generator (PRNG). We propose two methods: the Dyson series-based method and the Euler-Maruyama (EM)-based method. In the former, we express the noise term via the Dyson series approximation of the time evolution operator, while in the latter, it is approximated using the EM time discretization. Both methods use the quantum linear systems solver to generate the amplitude-encoding state of $\mathbf{X}_t$, making only ${\rm polylog}(N)$ queries to the PRNG circuit and the block-encodings of $A$ and $B$. Additionally, going beyond state preparation, we present methods to estimate expectations of functions of $\mathbf{X}_t$ using the state.
summary: This paper presents new quantum algorithms for solving high-dimensional linear stochastic differential equations. By using amplitude encoding for the noise term and a quantum PRNG, the authors achieve an exponential speed-up in the system dimension N. They propose two distinct approaches: one based on the Dyson series and another based on the Euler-Maruyama discretization. The work also provides methods for estimating expectations of functions of the solution at various time points.
main_problem: Developing quantum algorithms to solve high-dimensional linear stochastic differential equations (SDEs) with an exponential speed-up in the dimension N, specifically addressing the challenge of amplitude encoding the noise term.
main_result: The paper proposes two methods (Dyson series-based and Euler-Maruyama-based) that achieve polylog(N) query complexity, providing an exponential speed-up in the dimension N compared to classical methods.
method: The algorithms utilize amplitude encoding of the noise term via a quantum pseudorandom number generator (PRNG), quantum linear systems solvers (QLSS), and block-encoding techniques like the Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU).
model_or_system: The model consists of high-dimensional linear SDEs of the form dX_t = A(t)X_t dt + B(t)dW_t, where X_t is an N-dimensional random process driven by m-dimensional Brownian motion.
key_observables: Expectations of functions of X_t at multiple time steps (history states) and expectations of quantities defined at the terminal time.
important_parameters: Dimension N, dimension of Brownian motion m, time interval [0, T], error tolerance epsilon, and stability/dissipativity parameter eta.
main_assumptions: Assumes access to oracles for arithmetic operations and data loading, a bounded norm for the drift matrix A(t), and a bounded noise coefficient B(t).
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic outline of the Dyson and EM-based methods; Figure 2 illustrates the quantum circuit for block-encoding a product of matrices; Figure 3 shows the circuit implementation of the operator.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SDE problem, presents two algorithmic approaches (Dyson-based and EM-based), details the construction of quantum oracles and block-encodings, provides rigorous error analysis and complexity bounds, and concludes with discussions on limitations and future extensions.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as SDEs are fundamental for modeling dissipative dynamics, and efficient quantum algorithms for high-dimensional stochastic processes are crucial for simulating complex quantum trajectories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Outlines of the proposed quantum algorithms, the Dyson series-based method and the EM-based method. The yellow boxes represent given quantum circuits, and the orange boxes are what we construct from them. The blue arrow means that the object at the start is used to construct the one at the end.

Figure 2: The quantum circuit for block-encoding of An · · · A1, a product of matrices A1, . . . , An, constructed with block-encodings of them. Here and hereafter, a wire and a box represent a quantum register (set of qubits) and a quantum gate, respectively, and a wire bypassing a box means that the corresponding register is not used in the gate.
Figure 3: The quantum circuit implementation of U′ ˜Φ.
Figure 4: The quantum circuit implementation of U ˜∆.
